name: Vampir
description: The Vampir (вампир) is a creature of Serbian folklore, considered a revenant – a deceased person who rises from their grave to prey upon the living. Unlike the romanticized versions often depicted in modern media, the Serbian Vampir is typically described as bloated, ruddy-faced, and often still bearing the signs of decay. They are believed to return to their former homes and torment their families, often draining their strength and causing illness or death. Protection against Vampiri involved garlic, hawthorn stakes, holy water, and religious rituals. The belief in Vampiri was widespread in rural Serbia, and elaborate rituals were performed to prevent the dead from rising and to destroy those that did. The term "vampire" itself is believed to have originated from the Serbian word "vampir," highlighting the creature's deep roots in Serbian folklore and its influence on the global vampire mythos. These creatures were not always considered inherently evil, but more as a consequence of improper burial rites, a life of sin, or being cursed. The fear of the Vampir was a significant aspect of Serbian culture, influencing funerary practices and social behaviors. The belief in their existence persisted well into the 20th century, demonstrating the enduring power of Serbian folklore.
subtitle: The Serbian Revenant Bloodsucker
appearance: The Vampir is often described as bloated, with a ruddy or darkened face. They may retain features of their former life, but are typically depicted as grotesque and decaying. Their eyes are often bloodshot, and their nails are long and sharp. Sometimes they are described as having a strong, foul odor, indicative of their decaying state. Unlike modern depictions, they are rarely portrayed as attractive or seductive.
habitat: Graveyards, former homes, crossroads, and other liminal spaces associated with death and the supernatural.
behavior: Vampiri typically return to their former homes to torment their families and drain their life force. They are often active at night and are said to be able to take the form of animals or objects, though this ability is limited. They are driven by a primal hunger and a desire to inflict suffering on the living. Their behavior is often erratic and unpredictable, reflecting their state of undeath.

keyMyths.title: The Case of Petar Blagojević
keyMyths.description: The case of Petar Blagojević in 1725 is one of the most well-documented early accounts of vampirism. Blagojević, a Serbian peasant, allegedly returned from the dead and killed nine people before being exhumed and impaled with a stake.

keyMyths.title: The Story of Arnold Paole
keyMyths.description: Arnold Paole, another well-known case from the early 18th century, supposedly became a Vampir after being attacked by one. His subsequent death and the deaths that followed were attributed to his vampiric nature, leading to his exhumation and staking.

sources.author: T.P. Vukanović
sources.description: A detailed ethnological study of South Slavic folklore, including beliefs about Vampiri.
sources.text: A detailed ethnological study of South Slavic folklore, including beliefs about Vampiri.
